Article.ArticleTitle: The neutralizing antibody titer correlate of COVID-19 risk in the COVID-19 variant immunologic landscape (COVAIL) trial was not modified by SARS-CoV-2 amino acid sequence distances.

Abstract.AbstractText: In the Coronavirus Variant Immunologic Landscape Trial (COVAIL) conducted in the United States in 2022-2023, 985 participants received a second COVID-19 booster with one of twelve monovalent or bivalent mRNA inserts. Pseudovirus serum inhibitory dilution 50% neutralizing antibody titer (nAb titer) measured two-weeks post booster significantly associated with lower COVID-19 incidence over six months follow-up in this trial. COVAIL investigators sequenced SARS-CoV-2 Spike amino acid sequences for all COVID-19 cases, with a sequence successfully obtained from 129 of 195 cases. For COVID-19 endpoint cases we calculated five distances of the case-causing sequence to a reference sequence, the first two physico-chemical weighted Hamming distances of Spike or receptor binding domain (RBD) to a participant's nearest Spike or RBD vaccine-insert sequence, and the other three estimated degrees of neutralizing antibody escape from the XBB.1.5 RBD strain calculated with deep mutational scanning. Hypothesizing that the nAb titer correlate of risk may have a stronger association with COVID-19 when focusing on COVID-19 infections more closely matched to the vaccine insert in Spike or RBD amino acid sequence or with lower RBD antibody escape score, we tested this hypothesis for the combined group receiving a monovalent Prototype (ancestral strain) booster (n = 143) and for the combined group receiving an Omicron-containing booster (n = 744). For both combined groups, the nAb titer correlate of risk did not significantly vary across any of the assessed sequence distances from the vaccine insert (all p-values >0.10), although RBD Hamming distance had point estimates consistent with a weakening correlate with distance, motivating further exploration in settings with greater antigenic heterogeneity. Indeed, statistical power was bounded by the limited antigenic variability of viruses infecting trial participants over the follow-up period (April 21, 2022 to May 25, 2023), which spanned only a 3.02-fold nAb titer range of differential sensitivity to sera from XBB.1.5-infected individuals. ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: NCT05289037.
Abstract.CopyrightInformation: Copyright © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.. All rights reserved.

Author.CollectiveName: Coronavirus Variant Immunologic Landscape Trial (COVAIL) Study Team

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declaration of competing interest The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Rachel Presti, Richard Rupp, Patricia Winokur, Lindsay N. Carpp, and Peter B. Gilbert report funding was provided by Leidos Biomed/NIH to their institutions. Lindsay N. Carpp and Peter B. Gilbert report funding was provided by the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases to their institution. Nadine Rouphael reports funding was provided by Flu Lab to her institution. Dr. Rouphael also reports relationships with Merck, Sanofi, Pfizer, Vaccine Company, Immorna, and NIH that include: funding grants; a relationship with Uptodate that includes: royalties or licenses; a relationship with Krog that includes: consulting or advisory (consulting fees to self); relationships with Virology Education and Medscape that include: speaking and lecture fees; relationships with Sanofi and Moderna that include: travel reimbursement; relationships with Moderna, Sanofi, Seqirus, and Pfizer that include: consulting or advisory (Advisory Boards); relationships with Emmes, ICON, BARDA, CyanVac, Imunon, and Micronon that include: consulting or advisory (Safety Committees); relationships with ARLG, CDC Pertussis Challenge, and UAB Immunology Institute that include: consulting or advisory (Advisory Boards); a relationship with DMID SMC for a Malaria Challenge Model that includes: consulting or advisory (DMID SMC); and a relationship with Georgia Research Alliance that includes: receipt of equipment, materials, drugs, medical writing, gifts or other services (to her institution). Angela R. Branche reports relationships with Merck, Moderna, Pfizer, Cyanvac, and Vaccitech that include: funding grants; relationships with GSK, Moderna, Sanofi, and Novavax that include: consulting or advisory; and relationships with Moderna and Sanofi that include: speaking and lecture fees. David J. Diemert reports relationships with Immorna, Sanofi, and Leidos Biomed/NIH that include: funding or grants. Ann R. Falsey reports relationships with CyanVac, Moderna, Pfizer, AstraZeneca, and Sanofi that include: funding grants; relationships with ADMA Biologics, GSK, Sanofi Pasteur, Merck, AstraZeneca, and Shionogi that include: consulting or advisory (consulting fees to self); and relationships with GSK, Moderna, and Sanofi that include: travel reimbursement. Sharon Frey reports having reviewed articles for Vaccine but does not serve in an editorial capacity. Dr. Frey also reports having been a site principal investigator for the phase 3 Moderna and Janssen SARS-CoV-2 trials. Jennifer Whitaker reports a relationship with Pfizer that includes: funding grants; and relationships with Cyanvac and EMMES that include: consulting or advisory (Data Safety Monitoring Committees). Susan Little reports relationships with Gilead Sciences and NIH that include: funding grants. Satoshi Kamidani reports relationships with Pfizer, Moderna, Bavarian Nordic, Sanofi, the NIH, and the CDC that include: funding grants. Emmanuel B. Walter reports relationships with Pfizer, Moderna, Seqirus, Najit Technologies, and Clinetic that include: funding grants; and relationships with Vaxcyte, Pfizer, Seqirus, ILiAD Biotechnologies, Shionogi (Data Safety Monitoring Board) that include: consulting or advisory. Richard M. Novak reports relationships with Moderna that includes: consulting or advisory and travel reimbursement. Richard Rupp reports relationships with Merck, Pfizer, Moderna, Sanofi, GSK, and Vaxcyte that include: funding grants (vaccine clinical trials); and relationships with Bharat Biotech and GSK that include: consulting or advisory (Data Safety Monitoring Board). Lisa A. Jackson reports relationships with the NIH and the CDC that include: funding grants. Tara M. Babu reports a relationship with Sanofi that includes: speaking and lecture fees and a relationship with Sanofi that includes: consulting or advisory (Advisory Board). Anne Luetkemeyer reports relationships with the NIH and Gilead that include: funding grants. Lilly C. Immergluck reports relationships with the NIH, Sanofi, and Pfizer that include: funding grants; site PI for vaccine trial; a relationship with American Academy of Pediatrics for service on the executive board; a relationship with Illinois Chapter American Academy of Pediatrics that includes: service on immunization task force and honorarium for speaking engagements; a relationship with the NIH (DMID) that includes: co-chair for protocol development on HIV vaccine trial; and a relationship with the NIH (DMID) that includes: consulting or advisory (Data Safety Monitoring Board for mpox vaccine trial). Rachel Presti reports relationships with Merck, GSK, and Moderna that include: funding grants. Martín Bäcker reports a relationship with Pfizer that includes: funding grants. Patricia Winokur reports relationships with Pfizer and Duke University that include: funding grants (vaccine clinical trials); and relationships with Cyanvac and Emmes Co. that include: consulting or advisory (Data Safety Monitoring Board). Paul A. Goepfert reports a relationship with the NIH that includes: funding grants. Dahlene N. Fusco reports relationships with Gilead Sciences and AXCELLA that include: consulting or advisory (Advisory Boards); and reports relationships with Gilead Sciences, Regeneron, MetroBiotech LLC, and the NIH (DMID COVAIL) that include: site PI for clinical trials. All other authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
